name: Invariants & colouring
description: Possibility puzzles cracked by a conserved quantity: checkerboard colouring, parity of flips and moves, permutation parity, and piece-count monovariants.
primer:
## Find a conserved quantity
- **Checkerboard colouring:** a domino always covers 1 light + 1 dark; removing two same-colour squares breaks the balance → impossible (opposite corners share colour). One of each colour → always tileable (Gomory).
- **Parity:** flipping two cups changes the up-count by an even number (0 even ≠ 15 odd); a knight flips colour each move (odd # moves can't return).
- **Permutation parity:** 15-puzzle reaches only even permutations → can't swap just 14&15.
- **Monovariant:** each chocolate break adds exactly one piece → 24 pieces need 23 breaks.

cards.term: Remove two diagonally opposite corner squares from a standard 8x8 chessboard, leaving 62 squares. Can the remaining board be perfectly tiled by 31 dominoes, each covering two adjacent squares?
choices: It depends on the domino sizes
Only if one square is left uncovered
cards.definition: Correct: No, it is impossible. Colour the board like a checkerboard: every domino covers one light and one dark square. The two opposite corners share a colour, so you are left with 32 of one colour and 30 of the other - an impossible balance.

cards.term: From an 8x8 chessboard you remove one dark square and one light square (any two of opposite colour). Can the remaining 62 squares always be tiled by 31 dominoes?
choices: Only if the two squares are adjacent
Only if they are in the same row
cards.definition: Correct: Yes, it is always possible. Removing one square of each colour keeps the 31-31 balance, and a classic theorem (Gomory's) guarantees a tiling always exists - imagine a closed snake-path through all 64 squares; the two removals split it into two even arcs.

cards.term: Can a 5x5 board (25 squares) be perfectly tiled by dominoes, each covering two adjacent squares?
choices: Only if you start from a corner
cards.definition: Correct: No, it is impossible. Each domino covers exactly two squares, so any tileable board must have an even number of squares. 25 is odd, so no tiling can exist - the simplest invariant of all.

cards.term: A knight starts on a corner of a chessboard. Each move it makes a normal knight's move. Can it return to its starting square after exactly 7 moves?
choices: Only if it visits the centre
Only if no square is repeated
Yes, but only on a larger board
cards.definition: Correct: No, it is impossible. A knight always moves from a light square to a dark square and vice versa, so its colour flips every move. After 7 (an odd number of) moves it is on the opposite colour from where it started, so it cannot be back on the starting square.

cards.term: Fifteen cups are placed on a table, all facing DOWN. In one move you must turn over exactly two cups (any two). Is it possible, after some sequence of moves, to have all fifteen cups facing UP?
choices: Yes, but only an even number of moves
Only if you may flip one cup
cards.definition: Correct: No, it is impossible. Turning over two cups changes the number of upward-facing cups by an even amount, so its parity never changes. You start with 0 cups up (even) and want 15 (odd) - impossible.

cards.term: You have a 4x6 rectangular chocolate bar scored into 24 unit squares. Each 'break' takes one piece and snaps it in a straight line into two pieces. What is the minimum number of breaks needed to reduce the whole bar to 24 separate unit squares?
cards.definition: Correct: 23. Every break increases the number of pieces by exactly one, no matter where you snap. Going from 1 piece to 24 pieces therefore always takes 24 - 1 = 23 breaks.

cards.term: In the classic 15-puzzle (a 4x4 frame with 15 sliding tiles and one gap), starting from the solved position, is it possible to reach a position that looks solved EXCEPT that tiles 14 and 15 are swapped?
choices: Only if you remove a tile
Yes, but only an even number of moves
cards.definition: Correct: No, it is impossible. Each legal slide is one transposition of tiles, and returning the gap to its corner requires an even number of slides - so only EVEN tile permutations are reachable. Swapping just 14 and 15 is a single (odd) transposition, hence impossible.

cards.term: Remove all FOUR corner squares from an 8x8 chessboard, leaving 60 squares. Can the remaining board be tiled by 30 dominoes?
choices: Only if one square is left uncovered
Only with dominoes of two sizes
cards.definition: Correct: Yes, it can be tiled. The four corners are two dark and two light squares, so the 30-30 colour balance is preserved (unlike removing two SAME-colour corners), and an explicit tiling exists. Balance plus a valid construction means it works.
